1: vayedaber YHWH el-Moshe ve'el-Aharon lemor: YHWH speaks to both Moses and Aaron — the census (Numbers 1) was addressed to Moses alone; the camp arrangement is addressed to both, since both priestly and civil leadership determine the spatial arrangement of the holy community around the sanctuary.
2: ish al-diglo be'otot leveit avotam: 'each man by his own standard [degel], with the signs [otot] of their fathers' houses.' Degel (standard/banner) — the large tribal-group flag; ot (sign/standard) — the smaller clan-level banner. The two-level flag system mirrors the two-level organizational structure of the census (mishpacha and beit av). Mineged saviv le'ohel mo'ed yachanu: 'they shall camp facing, from all sides, the tent of meeting' — the tabernacle is the center; all four camp-directions face inward toward it. YHWH's dwelling is the centripetal force of Israel's entire spatial organization.
3: vehaconim kidmah mizracha degel machaneh Yehudah letzivotam: 'on the east side toward the sunrise shall be the standard of the camp of Judah by their companies.' Kidmah mizracha (eastward, toward the sunrise) — the most honorable position: the side of sunrise, the direction of the divine presence (the east-facing tabernacle entrance). Judah receives the vanguard position — first in the order of march, first in the direction of first light. This primacy of Judah (the messianic tribe) in the camp arrangement mirrors Jacob's deathbed blessing (Genesis 49:8-12).
4: utzva'o ufekudeihem arba'ah veshiv'im elef veshesh me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 74,600' — Judah's count repeated from Numbers 1. The repetition confirms: the census and camp arrangement are a single coordinated system. Tzava (company/army) — each tribal group is also an tzava, an army division in the marching order.
5: vehachoneh alav matteh Yissachar: 'and camping next to him shall be the tribe of Issachar.' Alav (next to him/alongside him) — the flanking position; Issachar and Zebulun flank Judah in the eastern camp. The three-tribe groupings of the four camps create four mini-armies, each under a lead tribe.
6: utzva'o ufekudav arba'ah vachamishim elef ve-arba me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 54,400' — Issachar's count from Numbers 1.
7: matteh Zevulun: the tribe of Zebulun. Zebulun completes the eastern camp group. Nafkei orach yam is Jacob's blessing (Genesis 49:13) — Zebulun's territory touches the sea; their later association with the Galilean coast region fulfills geographic destiny.
8: utzva'o ufekudav shiv'ah vachamishim elef ve-arba me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 57,400' — Zebulun's count.
9: kol hapekudim lemachaneh Yehudah me'at shmonah veshmonim elef ve-arba me'ot letzivotam rishonah yis'u: 'all those enrolled of the camp of Judah: 186,400 by their companies. They shall set out first.' Rishonah yis'u (they shall set out/march first) — Judah leads the entire nation in the march. This vanguard position for Judah is the spatial enactment of the messianic promise: the scepter-tribe goes first, the royal lineage at the head of the covenant nation.
10: degel machaneh Reuven teimana letzivotam: 'the standard of the camp of Reuben on the south side.' Teiman (south) — the second camp position. Despite being Jacob's firstborn, Reuben is assigned the secondary south position rather than the primary east. Reuben lost his primacy through the sin against Bilhah (Genesis 35:22; Numbers 49:3-4: 'unstable as water, you shall not have preeminence'). The census/camp arrangement encodes the covenantal consequences of the patriarchal history.
11: utzva'o ufekudav shisha ve-arba'im elef vachamesh me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 46,500' — Reuben's count. Reuben's group (46,500 + 59,300 + 45,650 = 151,450) is the second-smallest camp contingent, after Ephraim's (108,100).
12: vehachoneh alav matteh Shim'on: 'and camping next to him shall be the tribe of Simeon.' Simeon flanks Reuben in the southern camp. Jacob's dying words to Simeon and Levi were harsh (Genesis 49:5-7: 'I will divide them in Jacob and scatter them in Israel'). Simeon's numbers will collapse by the second census (from 59,300 to 22,200), and the tribe is not mentioned in Moses's final blessing (Deut 33). Levi's scattering becomes priestly blessing; Simeon's scattering becomes tribal dissolution.
13: utzva'o ufekudav tish'ah vachamishim elef ushlosh me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 59,300' — Simeon's count.
14: umatteh Gad: and the tribe of Gad. Gad rounds out the southern camp group with Reuben and Simeon. Gad's blessing (Genesis 49:19: 'raiders shall raid him, but he shall raid at their heels') suggests a warrior tribe; Gad will later settle east of the Jordan (Num 32) and be known for swift warriors (1 Chronicles 12:8).
15: utzva'o ufekudav chamisha vechamishim elef veshesh me'ot vachamishim: 'his company as enrolled: 45,650' — Gad's count.
16: kol hapekudim lemachaneh Reuven echad vachamishim elef ve-arba me'ot vachamishim letzivotam ushniim yis'u: 'all those enrolled of the camp of Reuben: 151,450 by their companies. They shall set out second.' Ushniim yis'u (they shall set out second) — Reuben's camp marches after Judah. The march order (east first, south second, west third, north last) maps the camp onto a counter-clockwise rotation when viewed from above.
17: venas'a ohel mo'ed machaneh haleviyim betoch hamachanot: 'then the tent of meeting shall set out, the camp of the Levites in the midst of the camps.' The tabernacle and its Levite carriers march in the center — between the two front camps (Judah, Reuben) and the two rear camps (Ephraim, Dan). The Levites are not a fighting unit but the center of the entire march. Ka'asher yachanu ken yis'u: 'as they camp, so they shall set out' — the marching formation exactly mirrors the camp arrangement. The holy center of Israel is always at the center, whether stationary or moving.
18: degel machaneh Efrayim letzivotam yama: 'the standard of the camp of Ephraim on the west side.' Yama (westward, toward the sea) — the third camp position. Ephraim's western position may reflect the tribal territory Ephraim would receive in Canaan (central highlands, western-ish). The western camp groups the Joseph tribes (Ephraim and Manasseh) with Benjamin — the three sons closest to Joseph in the Jacob narrative.
19: utzva'o ufekudav arba'ah vethlashim elef vachamesh me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 40,500' — Ephraim's count.
20: ve'alav matteh Menashe: 'and next to him the tribe of Manasseh.' Manasseh, despite seniority, is flanking rather than leading in the Joseph camp group — the Jacob blessing precedence (Ephraim over Manasseh, Gen 48) is encoded in the camp arrangement.
21: utzva'o ufekudav shnayim uthlashim elef umayatayim: 'his company as enrolled: 32,200' — Manasseh's count, the smallest in the census.
22: umatteh Binyamin: and the tribe of Benjamin. Benjamin joins the western camp with the Joseph tribes — his brothers by Rachel. Benjamin's physical closeness to the Joseph tribes in the patriarchal narrative (Benjamin = Rachel's second son, Joseph's full brother) is preserved in the camp arrangement. The brotherhood of full siblings is honored spatially.
23: utzva'o ufekudav chamisha uthlashim elef ve-arba me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 35,400' — Benjamin's count.
24: kol hapekudim lemachaneh Efrayim shmona vethlashim elef ume'ah letzivotam vshlishim yis'u: 'all those enrolled of the camp of Ephraim: 108,100 by their companies. They shall set out third.' Vshlishim yis'u (they shall set out third). The western camp (108,100) is the smallest of the four camps — Rachel's descendants plus Benjamin. The two sons of Jacob's beloved Rachel (Joseph/Ephraim-Manasseh and Benjamin) lead the western camp that marches third.
25: degel machaneh Dan tzafonah letzivotam: 'the standard of the camp of Dan on the north side.' Tzafonah (northward) — the fourth and last camp position. Dan leads the rear guard — the most rearward and exposed position. Dan's later association with idolatry and northern apostasy (Judg 17-18; 1 Kings 12:29) may connect to this end-of-the-line position at the furthest remove from the tabernacle.
26: utzva'o ufekudav shnayim veshish'im elef vesheva me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 62,700' — Dan's count. Dan is the second-largest single tribe (after Judah).
27: vehachoneh alav matteh Asher: 'and camping next to him the tribe of Asher.' Asher's prosperous blessing (Genesis 49:20; Deuteronomy 33:24-25) and the tribe's later territory in the northern coastal plain. Anna the prophetess from Asher (Luke 2:36-38) is the only NT figure identified by Asher tribal membership.
28: utzva'o ufekudav echad ve-arba'im elef vachamesh me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 41,500' — Asher's count.
29: umatteh Naftali: and the tribe of Naphtali, completing the northern rear-guard camp. Naphtali's blessing (Genesis 49:21: 'a swift deer that yields beautiful fawns') and territory in upper Galilee (Sea of Galilee region) connect to the Galilean ministry of Jesus (Matthew 4:13-16). The last camp group in the march contains the tribe whose land will be the first to see 'a great light.'
30: utzva'o ufekudav shloshah vachamishim elef ve-arba me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 53,400' — Naphtali's count.
31: kol hapekudim lemachaneh Dan me'at vashiv'im vethlashim elef vashmona me'ot le'acharonah yis'u lediegleihem: 'all those enrolled of the camp of Dan: 157,600. They shall set out last, by their standards.' Le'acharonah yis'u (they shall set out last) — the rear guard. Lediegleihem (by their standards) — the closing phrase of the marching-order section; the degel system provides organizational identity even to those at the back.
32: eleh pekudei vnei Yisrael leveit avotam: 'these are those enrolled of the people of Israel by their fathers' houses.' The census summary restates the organizing principle: beit avot (fathers' houses). The entire camp is structured on patriarchal lineage — each person knows their place in the spatial arrangement because they know their tribal ancestry.
33: vehaleviyim lo hitpakedu betoch bnei Yisrael ka'asher tzivah YHWH et-Moshe: 'and the Levites were not enrolled among the people of Israel, as YHWH commanded Moses.' The Levite exemption from the twelve-tribe census is confirmed at the chapter's close. Ka'asher tzivah YHWH — as YHWH commanded: the exception is not an oversight but deliberate divine structuring. The Levites stand apart — spatially (at the center), functionally (sanctuary service), and statistically (not counted in the military census).
34: va-ya'asu bnei Yisrael kekol asher tzivah YHWH et-Moshe ken chanu letiegleihem vachen nas'u ish lemishpechotav al-beit avotav: 'the people of Israel did according to all that YHWH commanded Moses. So they camped by their standards, and so they set out, each one by his clan, according to his father's house.' The closing obedience formula mirrors Numbers 1:54. Both the census and the camp arrangement are completed in full obedience. Ish lemishpechotav al-beit avotav (each one by his clan, according to his father's house) — the final phrase returns to the organizing categories of Numbers 1:2. Order, genealogy, and obedience characterize Israel at Sinai — before the wilderness failures begin.
